what: what are the resolve and reject functions
why: why do we pass resolve and reject to a promise

1_beginner_explanation.summary: `resolve` and `reject` are two functions provided to you by JavaScript when you create a `new Promise`. They are your 'controls' for the Promise. You call `resolve(data)` when your async task finishes successfully — this hands the data to any attached `.then()`. You call `reject(error)` when the task fails — this hands the error to any attached `.catch()`. Until you call one of them, the Promise remains stuck in the 'pending' state.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine `resolve` and `reject` as two different stamps on a shipping box. If you stamp it `resolve`, it goes onto the delivery truck (the `.then` handlers). If you stamp it `reject`, it goes to the damaged goods department (the `.catch` handlers). If you forget to stamp it entirely, the box sits in the warehouse forever (pending).

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When `resolve(value)` is called, the JS engine sets the Promise's internal state to 'fulfilled' and queues all attached `onFulfilled` handlers into the Microtask Queue. When `reject(reason)` is called, it sets the state to 'rejected' and queues all `onRejected` handlers. Both functions only accept a single argument. Any additional arguments are ignored.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: The `value` passed to `resolve` or `reject` is stored in the Promise's internal `[[PromiseResult]]` slot, keeping the object/error alive in memory so it can be passed to future `.then()` or `.catch()` attachments.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Wrapping an older callback-based API (like `setTimeout` or `Geolocation`) into a modern Promise using resolve and reject.

4_common_confusion.trap: Forgetting to call `resolve` or `reject` entirely, or placing them inside an `if` block that doesn't cover all conditions. The Promise will stay 'pending' forever, creating a memory leak and hanging the application.
4_common_confusion.fix: Always ensure every logical path in your Promise executor eventually calls either `resolve` or `reject`.

6_edge_case.quirk: If you pass a Promise into `resolve()`, the outer Promise will adopt the state of the inner Promise. It will wait for the inner Promise to settle, and then settle with the same state and value.
